slides.title: Buod ng Ikatlong Linggo
slides.text: Binabati kita! Natapos mo ang lahat ng slide sa linggong ito. Natutunan natin ang kahulugan ng pag-iimpok at pagtitipid. Nalaman natin ang pagtatabi ng barya sa alkansyang kawayan. Natutunan natin ang pagtitipid sa pagkain, tubig, at ilaw. Iniwasan natin ang pagsasayang ng yaman at maling pagbili. Gumawa tayo ng plano ng pagtitipid kasama ang pamilya. Handa ka na ba sa pagsusulit at hamon ng linggong ito? Ipagpatuloy ang pagiging matipid at disiplinadong bata! Halimbawa, tingnan natin ang tungkol sa Tandaan: Pag-iimpok = Pera para sa bukas. Pagtitipid = Matalinong paggamit. Tipirin ang yaman! Upang maunawaan ang konseptong ito nang malalim, kailangan nating tingnan kung paano ito nalalapat sa iba't ibang sitwasyon sa ating pang-araw-araw na buhay. Sa pamamagitan ng pag-aaral ng paksang ito, ang mga mag-aaral ay maaaring bumuo ng mga kasanayan sa kritikal na pag-iisip.

examples.title: Tandaan
examples.content: Pag-iimpok = Pera para sa bukas. Pagtitipid = Matalinong paggamit. Tipirin ang yaman!

standard.q: Ano ang tawag sa pagtatabi ng labis na barya o pera para sa hinaharap?
Ano ang tawag sa matalino at maingat na paggamit ng mga likas na yaman?
Saan karaniwang inilalagay ng mga bata ang kanilang naimpok na barya sa bahay?
options: sa alkansya
sa ilalim ng pader

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang nagpapakita ng pagtitipid sa kuryente?
options: hayaang nakabukas ang bentilador kapag walang tao
patayin ang ilaw at bentilador kapag lalabas ng silid-aralan
isaksak ang telebisyon buong gabi
gamitin ang lamp kahit maliwanag ang araw

standard.q: Paano mo maiiwasan ang pagsasayang ng pagkain sa inyong hapag-kainan?
options: kumuha ng napakaraming pagkain at magtira sa plato
kumuha lamang ng sapat na sukat at ubusin ang nasa plato
itapon ang kanin sa basurahan

standard.q: Ano ang dapat gawin sa gripo habang nagsesepilyo ng ngipin sa banyo?
options: hayaang nakabukas at tumutulo ang tubig
isara ang gripo at gumamit ng baso
sirain ang gripo
maglaro ng tubig sa gripo

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang nagpapakita ng pagtitipid sa mga gamit sa eskwela?
options: punitin ang mga blankong papel sa notebook
gamitin ang lapis hanggang sa ito ay maging munti nang may pag-iingat
itapon ang krayola kapag nabali ang dulo
bumili ng bagong bag buwan-buwan

standard.q: Sino ang makikinabang kapag ang buong pamilya ay nagtutulungan sa pag-iimpok at pagtitipid?
options: ang kapitan ng barangay lamang
ang buong pamilya at magulang
ang mga pulis sa outpost lamang
ang mga tindero sa palengke

standard.q: Ano ang angkop na gamitin sa paliligo upang makatipid sa tubig kumpara sa shower?
options: balde at tabo
gripo na walang tigil ang tulo

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang pagtitipid ng kuryente sa pamamagitan ng pagpatay ng ilaw ay nakatutulong sa badyet ng pamilya.
Ano ang dapat gawin sa mga tirang pagkain na maaari pang kainin sa susunod na kainan?
options: itapon sa trash bin agad
itabi nang maayos sa refrigerator upang maiinit muli
ipakain sa aso ng kapitbahay
iwanan sa mesa nang walang takip

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang pambalana sa pook kung saan tayo nag-iimpok ng pera sa komunidad?
options: bangko o alkansya

standard.q: Anong materyal sa kalikasan ang karaniwang ginagamit sa paggawa ng katutubong alkansya?
options: kawayan o luwad

standard.q: Punan ang patlang: 'Ang bawat ___ ng naimpok na barya ay may halaga para sa hinaharap.'
options: piso o barya
patak ng luho

standard.q: Ano ang dapat mong gawin kapag nais mong bumili ng bagong laruan ngunit may laruan ka pa sa bahay?
options: umiyak sa harap ng nanay upang ibili ka
magtiyaga sa lumang laruan at iimpok ang barya sa alkansya
sirain ang lumang laruan
manghingi ng pera sa kapitbahay

standard.q: Saan maaaring gamitin ang pinaghugasan ng bigas sa bahay upang makatipid sa tubig?
options: itapon sa sahig ng kusina
ipangdilig sa mga halaman sa hardin
ipanghugas ng mukha
itapon sa basura

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang pagbili ng mga mamahaling gamit na hindi kailangan sa paaralan ay bahagi ng pagtitipid.
Aling ugali ang ipinapakita ng batang nagsusulat sa likod ng mga lumang papel na malinis pa?
options: pagkamatipid at pagpapahalaga sa gamit

standard.q: Ano ang angkop na sabihin sa kapatid na hinayaang nakabukas ang TV kahit walang nanonood?
options: 'Hayaan mo lang yan para maingay.'
'Patayin natin ang telebisyon upang makatipid tayo sa kuryente.'
'Ilipat natin sa cartoon kahit matutulog na tayo.'
'Huwag mong hawakan ang TV.'

standard.q: Ano ang pangunahing benepisyo ng pag-iimpok para sa isang Grade 3 student?
options: makabili ng pinakamataas na tatak ng sapatos
makabili ng sariling gamit sa eskwela sa pasukan nang hindi umaasa sa magulang
maging sikat sa klase
makabili ng computer agad

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang tamang kilos kapag nakita mong umaapaw ang tubig sa drum ng barangay?
options: paglaruan ang tubig na umaapaw
isara ang gripo nang kusa upang hindi masayang ang tubig
huwag pansinin dahil hindi mo naman drum ito
isumbong sa bumbero agad

standard.q: Tukuyin ang pambalana sa bagay na nagbibigay-liwanag sa desk na ginagamitan ng kuryente:
options: lamp o ilaw

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang pagkusa sa pagpatay ng ilaw kapag umaalis sa silid ay nagpapakita ng disiplina sa sarili.
Alin sa mga sumusunod ang nagpapakita ng tamang pagtitipid sa pagkain sa paaralan?
options: magbaon ng napakaraming pagkain at itapon ang hindi naubos
magbaon ng sapat na pagkain at ubusin ito sa oras ng recess
bumili ng junk food sa labas ng gate
huwag kumain ng recess

standard.q: Saan dapat magsimula ang pagtuturo ng pagtitipid at pag-iimpok?
options: sa banko lamang
sa loob ng tahanan kasama ang pamilya araw-araw
sa palengke lamang
kapag malaki na at nagtatrabaho na

challenge.q: Challenge: Kung ang isang pamilya ay patuloy na nagsasayang ng kuryente at tubig, ano ang maaaring mangyari sa kanilang badyet?
options: Lalaki ang kanilang babayarang bill na magdudulot ng kakulangan sa pera para sa pagkain at iba pang pangangailangan.
Mababawasan ang kanilang bayarin sa bangko.
Mas magiging masaya ang Mayor ng bayan.
Walang magiging epekto ito sa pamilya.

challenge.q: Challenge: Paano nakatutulong ang pag-iimpok ng barya sa alkansya sa paghahanda para sa mga hindi inaasahang pangyayari (emergencies)?
options: Ang naimpok na pera ay magagamit upang ibili ng gamot o kagamitan sa oras ng biglaang pangangailangan nang hindi nangungutang.
Ang pera ay mabubulok sa loob ng kawayan.
Hindi na kailangang magtrabaho ng mga magulang.
Maaaring ipamigay ang pera sa mga kalaro sa plaza.

challenge.q: Challenge: Aling paraan ang pinakamahusay upang makatipid sa pagbili ng mga aklat sa paaralan kung may kapatid kang katatapos lang sa Grade 3?
options: bumili ng bago at mamahaling aklat sa mall
gamitin at ingatan ang mga lumang aklat ng kapatid na maaari pang basahin
huwag nang mag-aklat kailanman sa klase
punitin ang mga aklat ng kapatid

challenge.q: Challenge: Bakit ang paggamit ng pinaghugasan ng bigas o gulay sa pagdidilig ng halaman ay itinuturing na matalinong pagtitipid?
options: Dahil nakakatipid ito sa paggamit ng malinis na tubig mula sa gripo sa pamamagitan ng pag-recycle ng tubig.
Dahil mas gusto ng halaman ang tubig-bigas kaysa sa ulan.
Dahil mas madali itong gawin kaysa sa paglilinis ng pader.
Dahil binabayaran ng barangay ang pamilya sa pag-recycle.

challenge.q: Challenge: Aling ugnayan ang pinakatumpak sa pagitan ng disiplina sa pag-iimpok at pagmamahal sa magulang?
options: Ang matipid na bata ay nagpapagaan sa pinansyal na gastusin ng kanyang mga magulang na nagpapakita ng pagmamahal at pag-aalaga sa pamilya.
Walang ugnayan ang pagtitipid ng anak sa pagmamahal sa magulang.
Nag-iimpok ang bata upang bigyan ng laruan ang kanyang sarili lamang.
Nagtitipid ang bata upang hindi na siya pakainin ng magulang.

performance.title: Aking Plano ng Pagtitipid sa Bahay
performance.desc: Gumawa ng isang simpleng listahan o tsart ng pagtitipid sa inyong bahay. Isulat ang tatlong paraan kung paano mo titipirin ang tubig at kuryente sa inyong tahanan (hal. pagpatay ng ilaw kapag walang tao, paggamit ng baso sa pagse-sepilyo). Ipakita ang tsart na ito sa iyong magulang at talakayin kung paano ito makatutulong sa badyet ng pamilya.
labels: Wastong natukoy ang tatlong paraan ng pagtitipid sa bahay
Maayos na naisulat at naiguhit ang tsart ng pagtitipid sa papel
Malinaw na naipaliwanag ang badyet at plano ng pagtitipid sa magulang

pages:
Pagsasanay 1: Pag-iimpok vs. Pagtitipid

Tukuyin kung ang sitwasyon ay nagpapakita ng Pag-iimpok (saving money) o Pagtitipid (conserving resources) gamit ang stylus.

1. Nagtabi ng limang piso si Matteo mula sa kanyang baon sa kanyang alkansya.
Pag-iimpok
2. Isinara ni Lani ang gripo habang sinasabon ang kanyang mga kamay.
Pagtitipid
3. Pinatay ni Kiko ang bentilador bago lumabas ng silid-aralan.
Pagtitipid

Pagsasanay 2: Matalinong Paggamit ng Yaman

Isulat kung ang gawi sa bahay ay Matipid (karapat-dapat) o Matalak (dapat iwasan/sinasayang ang yaman).

1. Paggamit ng pinaghugasan ng bigas sa pagdidilig ng halaman.
Matipid
2. Hayaang nakasaksak ang TV kahit natutulog na ang lahat.
Matalak
3. Kumuha ng sapat na pagkain lamang at ubusin ang nasa plato.
Matipid

Pagsasanay 3: Pagpaplano ng Pag-iimpok

Sagutin ang tanong gamit ang tumpak na detalye batay sa iyong sariling alkansya at aralin.

Bakit mahalagang mag-impok ng barya ang isang bata sa kanyang alkansya araw-araw?
Upang may magamit na sariling pera sa oras ng pangangailangan o pambili ng gamit sa eskwela.
Nakatutulong din ito upang matutong maging disiplinado at matipid sa paglipas ng panahon.

slides.title: Story: The Honest Woodcutter - Part 1
slides.text: Once upon a time, a poor woodcutter lived in a small cottage near a deep forest. He worked hard every day, cutting down old trees for firewood to feed his family. One afternoon, as he was chopping a tree branch near the bank of a deep, fast-flowing river, his iron axe slipped from his sweaty hands and splashed into the water. The woodcutter sat by the muddy bank and began to weep. He was very sad because the river was deep, and he could not swim. The iron axe was his only tool, and he could not afford to buy a new one. Without his axe, he could not earn any money to buy food. Suddenly, a beautiful water spirit rose up from the bubbles. The woodcutter was very poor but worked hard every day to support his family. He walked to the forest early in the morning and spent hours cutting wood. When his axe fell into the deep river, he felt helpless because he did not have money to buy a new one, and without it, he could not earn a living.

slides.title: Story: The Honest Woodcutter - Part 2
slides.text: The water spirit saw his tears and asked, 'Why are you crying, my friend?' The woodcutter explained his trouble. Hearing this, the spirit dove down into the deep water and returned holding a shining golden axe. 'Is this yours?' she asked. 'No,' replied the honest woodcutter. 'That is not my axe.' The spirit dove again and returned with a beautiful silver axe. 'Is this yours?' she asked. 'No,' he said. Finally, she dove a third time and brought up his old, rusty iron axe. 'Yes! That is mine!' he cried happily. The spirit smiled. She was so pleased with his honesty that she decided to reward him. 'Take all three axes,' she said, placing them in his hands. The woodcutter was surprised to see a beautiful water fairy rise from the river, holding a shining golden axe. The fairy asked if the gold axe was his, but the honest woodcutter immediately said no. He did not let the sight of wealth tempt him, choosing to tell the truth instead of taking something that was not his.

slides.title: Story: The Honest Woodcutter - Part 3
slides.text: The woodcutter thanked the spirit and returned to his village with the gold, silver, and iron axes. He was now rich, but he remained a humble and kind man. A greedy neighbor heard his story and wanted to get rich too. He went to the river bank, threw his iron axe into the water, and began crying. The water spirit rose up and dove down, bringing a golden axe. 'Is this yours?' she asked. 'Yes, yes! That is mine!' lied the greedy neighbor, reaching for it. The spirit was angry at his lie. She took the golden axe and disappeared under the water, leaving the greedy neighbor with nothing, not even his own iron axe. He walked home empty-handed and sad. The fairy was so pleased with the woodcutter's honesty that she gave him all three axes as a reward. The woodcutter returned home happily and shared the good news with his family. He proved that honesty is always the best policy, and that being truthful brings respect, peace of mind, and unexpected rewards in the end.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Woodcutter - A person who cuts down trees for firewood.
• Wept - Cried past tense.
• Afford - Have enough money to pay for something.

Observe how the prefix 'wood-' and suffix '-cutter' combine to form a new noun!

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. Why did the woodcutter cry?
2. What three axes did the water spirit bring up?
3. Why did the water spirit reward him with all three axes?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Honesty is the best policy.

The spirit was so pleased with the woodcutter's honesty that she gave him all three axes as a reward. Honesty brings respect and true rewards, whereas greed can lose us even what we have.

Discuss: When is a time you chose to tell the truth?

slides.title: Poem: The Whispering Wind - Part 1
slides.text:
I hear the wind go whispering by,
Under the blue and open sky.
It tells a tale of distant trees,
And dancing leaves and busy bees.

When I walk outside in the afternoon, I can feel a gentle breeze brushing against my cheeks. The wind makes a soft whistling sound as it moves through the green leaves of the big mango trees. It seems to carry stories from far-away places that I have never visited. The leaves dance and shake, and the colorful flowers nod their heads as if they are listening to the wind's secrets. It is a peaceful, beautiful sight that makes me feel happy and relaxed. I stand still and listen closely to the whispering sounds of nature around me, enjoying the calm and quiet day. The wind's whispers are a gentle reminder of the beauty and life that surrounds us in nature. It travels over mountains and seas, carrying seeds and helping plants reproduce. It also helps cool the hot earth, bringing comfort to animals and people. We can learn a lot by listening quietly to the sounds of nature.

slides.title: Poem: The Whispering Wind - Part 2
slides.text:
It travels far, it travels wide,
With secrets locked up deep inside.
It has no hands, it has no feet,
Yet sings a song so soft and sweet.

The wind is a mysterious traveler. It can cross high mountains, fly over deep oceans, and run through busy city streets without ever stopping. It has no physical body, so we cannot see it with our eyes, but we can see the results of its work. It pushes sailboats across the water and lifts colorful kites high into the air. When it is gentle, it sings a sweet song, rustling the grass. But when it is strong, it can roar like a lion. The wind is a powerful force of nature that connects all parts of the world. It is always moving, bringing changes to the weather. The wind also plays with the colorful kites that children fly in the park, lifting them high into the blue sky. It fills the sails of boats, helping them travel across the lakes and oceans. The wind is a powerful but invisible force of nature that helps us in many different ways every day.

slides.title: Poem: The Whispering Wind - Part 3
slides.text:
It cools the earth on hot summer days,
And pushes clouds in gentle ways.
So listen close when breezes blow,
And learn the secrets winds may know.

During hot afternoons, the cool breeze is a wonderful gift. It helps lower the temperature and makes us feel refreshed. The wind also plays a major role in our weather, pushing clouds across the sky to bring rain to thirsty crops. Scientists study the wind's speed and direction to predict the weather. By observing how the wind moves, we learn about physics and earth science. So next time you feel a breeze, close your eyes and listen to the whispering wind, appreciating the natural wonders of our environment. It has many lessons to teach us if we pay attention. The wind's cycle is a natural part of our weather system, bringing rain clouds to water the fields and clean the air. It works together with the sun and water to keep our planet green and healthy. By observing the wind, we can understand the science of weather and appreciate the delicate balance of our environment.

slides.title: Rhyme Scheme & Phonics
slides.text:
Phonics Focus: Rhyming Words
Let's identify the rhyming pairs in the poem:
• by / sky
• trees / bees
• wide / inside
• feet / sweet

Rhymes help create a natural rhythm in poems.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. What color is the sky in the poem?
2. Name two things the wind tells tales about.
3. What secrets does the wind carry?

slides.title: Moral Focus: Observation
slides.text:
Nature is full of quiet beauty. By slowing down and listening to the wind or observing the leaves, we learn to appreciate the world around us.

Discuss: Close your eyes for one minute. What sounds of nature can you hear around you right now?

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun - Part 1
slides.text: Welcome to our final speech gym challenge! Today we will visit a beautiful sandy seashore where a girl named Sally sells shells. Sally sells seashells by the seashore. The shells she sells are surely seashells. So if she sells shells on the seashore, I'm sure she sells seashore shells. Let's try saying this together: 'She sells seashells by the seashore.' Make sure you make a clean distinction between the soft /s/ sound in 'sells' and the soft /sh/ sound in 'shells'. Say it three times, starting slowly and then speeding up! It is a great exercise to train your tongue and lips for clear pronunciation and enunciation. Speak clearly to show your confidence. To make a clean sound, practice repeating these words slowly with your classmates. Sally worked hard to collect the most beautiful shells, placing them carefully in her basket. She wanted to show them to her friends and teach them about the different types of sea creatures that live in the ocean, sharing her knowledge.

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun - Part 2
slides.text: Next, let's meet our old friend Peter Piper again. He is picking peppers in the garden. Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers. Did Peter Piper pick a peck of pickled peppers? If Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers, where's the peck of pickled peppers Peter Piper picked? A peck is a unit of dry measure, about two gallons! That is a lot of peppers. Let's practice saying the 'P' sounds: 'Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers.' Make sure your lips pop clearly for each 'P' sound. Try saying it three times rapidly without stumbling or mixing up the words. Polly the puppy watched Peter and barked happily at him. Practicing these tongue twisters is a fun way to improve our pronunciation and speaking skills. It helps us speak clearly when we read books, ask questions in class, or tell stories to our family. Remember to focus on the different sounds, letting your tongue and lips move smoothly and accurately as you practice.

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun - Part 3
slides.text: Finally, let's try a new twister about silly sheep sleeping in a shed. Six silly sheep slept soundly in the wooden shed. The sheep should sleep in the shed, not in the grass. Let's say: 'Six silly sheep slept soundly in the shed.' Say it as fast as you can! If you make a mistake, don't worry. Just take a deep breath, slow down, and try again. Practicing these tongue twisters daily helps improve your reading speed, speech clarity, and verbal confidence. Announcers, actors, and teachers practice them to speak clearly. You are doing a wonderful job. Keep up the good work and have fun with speech! Practice makes perfect. Speech exercises also help us build confidence when speaking in front of others. By practicing regularly, we can overcome our speaking difficulties and express our ideas clearly and effectively. Don't be afraid to make mistakes; just slow down, breathe, and try again, showing determination and patience as you learn. Clear speaking is a superpower that helps us communicate better.

slides.title: Speech & Pronunciation Practice
slides.text:
Phonics Focus: S-blend / Sh-sound contrast

Tongue twisters help train our vocal cords and tongue muscles to enunciate clearly. Practice saying 'sells' (soft /s/) and 'shells' (soft /sh/) back-to-back.

slides.title: Comprehension & Speech Tasks
slides.text:
Practice Activities:
1. Say Twister 1 three times, slowly increasing your speed.
2. Say Twister 2 without stumbling on the 'P' sound.
3. What does 'peck' mean? (A unit of dry measure).

slides.title: Focus: Perseverance
slides.text:
Learning to speak clearly takes practice. Don't worry if you slip up; tongue twisters are meant to be challenging! Keep trying and have fun with it.

Discuss: Why is it important to speak slowly and clearly when giving a speech?

thursday.title: Dr. Jose Rizal: The Avid Reader

slides.title: Biography: Dr. Jose Rizal - Part 1
slides.text: Dr. Jose Rizal is the national hero of the Philippines, and he was an exceptionally smart and curious boy. When he was young, his family called him Pepe. Pepe lived in Koronadal, Timog Cotabato, and he loved to read books more than playing outside. Back then, books were rare and expensive. Pepe's mother, Teodora Alonso, was his very first teacher. She taught him how to read the alphabet and read him stories every night under the warm light of an oil lamp. Pepe listened carefully to every word, fascinated by the stories of distant places and heroes. He realized that reading was the key to understanding the world. He read every single book he could find. Jose's mother was his first teacher, and she taught him how to read the alphabet and pray. She noticed his love for books and encouraged him to read stories that taught moral lessons. Jose spent hours reading, letting his imagination travel to distant lands and learning about different cultures and ideas from around the world.

slides.title: Biography: Dr. Jose Rizal - Part 2
slides.text: One quiet evening, Pepe's mother read him a famous fable about a young moth. The young moth was fascinated by the bright, glowing flame of a candle. The mother moth warned her child: 'Do not fly too close to the flame, my child, or your wings will burn!' But the young moth did not listen. It was attracted to the light, flew too close, and its wings burned, causing it to fall into the hot oil. Pepe sat in silence, watching the real moths fly around their oil lamp. He never forgot this story. He learned that we must listen to the wise advice of our parents and teachers to keep ourselves safe from danger. He was a good boy. Jose was a very bright student who loved to learn languages, science, and history. He believed that education was the key to helping his country and improving the lives of his people. He studied hard in different schools and universities, always seeking to gain new knowledge and skills that he could use to help others.

slides.title: Biography: Dr. Jose Rizal - Part 3
slides.text: As Pepe grew up, his love for reading and learning never stopped. He went to school in Manila and later traveled to Europe to study medicine, becoming a skilled eye doctor. He also learned to speak over twenty languages! He wrote two famous novels, 'Noli Me Tangere' and 'El Filibusterismo', which helped show the difficulties of the Filipino people and inspired them to seek independence. He became a great artist, writer, and hero. Jose Rizal proved that reading books can open up the entire world to us and give us the power to help our country. He is an inspiration for all Filipino children to study hard. We honor him today. Jose Rizal's books and writings inspired the Filipino people to unite and fight for their freedom and rights. He remains a national hero because of his patriotism, intelligence, and dedication to education. His story teaches us that reading and learning are powerful tools that can change our lives and help our country succeed.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• National Hero - A person recognized for their exceptional contribution to their country.
• Attracted - Drawn or pulled towards something.
• Advice - Recommendations or guidance offered.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. Who was Pepe's first teacher?
2. What animal was the main character in the story Pepe's mother read?
3. What did Pepe learn from the story of the moth?

slides.title: Focus: Lifelong Learning
slides.text:
Dr. Jose Rizal became a doctor, a writer, an artist, and a hero because he never stopped reading and learning. Books open up the entire world to us.

Discuss: Like Pepe, what is a story your parents or teachers told you that you still remember?

slides.title: Story: The Boy Who Cried Wolf - Part 1
slides.text: Once, a young shepherd boy was given the duty of tending a flock of sheep on a grassy hill near a quiet forest. The job was lonely, and the boy got very bored sitting on the hill all day with nothing to do. To amuse himself, he decided to play a trick on the villagers. He ran down towards the village, waving his arms and shouting at the top of his lungs, 'Wolf! Wolf! A wolf is chasing the sheep!' The villagers stopped their work, grabbed sticks and axes, and rushed up the hill to help him. When they arrived, they found the sheep grazing peacefully, and the boy laughed at them. The villagers were very angry at him. The boy felt very lonely and bored on the hillside, watching the sheep eat grass all day. He wanted some excitement and attention, so he decided to play a trick on the villagers. He did not think about the consequences of his actions, choosing to tell a lie just to see the people run to help.

slides.title: Story: The Boy Who Cried Wolf - Part 2
slides.text: The villagers warned the boy, 'Do not cry wolf when there is no wolf!' They went back to their work. However, a few days later, the shepherd boy got bored again. He decided to play the same trick. He ran down the hill screaming, 'Wolf! Wolf!' The villagers rushed up the hill again. Once more, they found no wolf, only the boy laughing at their worried faces. 'A liar will not be believed, even when he tells the truth!' warned the wise village elder. The boy just ignored the warning and kept laughing. Then, one afternoon, a real, hungry wolf came out from the dark forest and attacked the sheep. The boy was very scared. The boy laughed at the angry villagers, thinking it was a very funny joke. He did not realize that by telling lies, he was losing their trust and respect. The villagers warned him that lying is wrong and that nobody believes a liar, even when he tells the truth. But the boy did not listen to their advice.

slides.title: Story: The Boy Who Cried Wolf - Part 3
slides.text: The boy was terrified. He ran towards the village, screaming louder than ever, 'Help! Wolf! Wolf! A real wolf is eating the sheep! Please help!' The villagers heard his cries, but they smiled and said to each other, 'He is just playing another trick to laugh at us. We will not go.' Nobody went up the hill to help him. The wolf killed many sheep and scattered the flock into the forest. The boy sat on the hill and cried, realizing his lies had cost him his flock. He learned that when we lie, we destroy the trust of others. Once trust is broken, people will not believe us even when we tell the truth. Honesty is very important. The boy shivered in fear as the wolf came closer, but his cries were ignored by the villagers. He realized too late that his lies had cost him the safety of his sheep and the trust of his community. He learned that honesty is essential, and that we must always tell the truth to keep our friends' trust.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Shepherd - A person who guards and tends sheep.
• Tending - Caring for or watching over.
• Amuse - Entertain or cause amusement.

Observe how 'shepherd' combines 'sheep' and 'herd'!

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. Why did the boy cry 'Wolf' when there was no wolf?
2. Why didn't the villagers come the third time?
3. What happened to the sheep at the end?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Trustworthiness.

A liar will not be believed, even when they speak the truth. Trust takes a long time to build but can be destroyed in a single moment.

Discuss: Why is it important for people to be able to trust your word?

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Langgam at ang Tipaklong - Bahagi 1
slides.text: Noong panahon ng tag-araw, ang buong kagubatan ay napakaganda at puno ng pagkain. Habang si Tipaklong ay masayang kumakanta at sumasayaw sa ilalim ng init ng araw, si Langgam naman ay abalang-abala sa paghahanap at paghahakot ng mga butil ng palay at prutas patungo sa kanyang munting bahay. 'Langgam, halika muna rito at magsaya tayo! Napakaraming pagkain ngayon, bakit ka nagpapapagod?' tanong ni Tipaklong habang tumutugtog ng gitara. Sumagot si Langgam, 'Nag-iipon ako ng pagkain para sa darating na tag-ulan upang hindi kami magutom. Dapat mag-ipon ka na rin, Tipaklong.' Ngunit tinawanan lamang siya ni Tipaklong at nagpatuloy sa paglalaro.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Langgam at ang Tipaklong - Bahagi 2
slides.text: Mabilis na lumipas ang mga araw at dumating ang panahon ng tag-ulan. Ulan dito, ulan doon, at naging napakalamig ng buong kagubatan. Nabasa ang mga dahon at walang makitang pagkain sa paligid dahil nalubog sa tubig ang mga damo. Ligtas na nanatili si Langgam sa loob ng kanyang tuyo at mainit na tahanan, kasama ang kanyang pamilya habang masayang kumakain ng mga butil na naipon nila noong tag-araw. Samantala, si Tipaklong ay gutom na gutom at nanginginig sa lamig sa labas, walang makitang masisilungan at walang makain.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Langgam at ang Tipaklong - Bahagi 3
slides.text: Dahil sa matinding gutom, lumapit si Tipaklong sa bahay ni Langgam at kumatok. 'Kaibigang Langgam, pakiusap at bigyan mo ako kahit kaunting pagkain. Nanginginig na ako sa gutom,' pakiusap niya. Nakaramdam ng awa si Langgam, kaya pinapasok niya si Tipaklong at binigyan ng mainit na pagkain at tuyong dahon. 'Salamat, Langgam. Ngayon ay naintindihan ko na kung bakit ka nagtrabaho noong tag-araw habang ako ay naglaro lamang,' wika ni Tipaklong nang may pagsisisi. Nangako si Tipaklong na sa susunod na tag-araw, mag-iipon din siya ng pagkain tulad ng kanyang masipag na kaibigan.

slides.title: Talasalitaan at Pag-aaral ng Salita
slides.text: Mga Bokabularyo: • Tag-araw - Panahon ng init at sikat ng araw. • Paghahakot - Pagsasama-sama o pagdadala ng mga bagay sa isang lugar. • Pagsisisi - Pakiramdam ng lungkot dahil sa maling desisyong ginawa. Tutok sa Phonics: Magsanay sa pagbigkas ng tunog ng 'l' sa 'langgam', 'lamig', at 'laro'.

slides.title: Mga Tanong sa Pag-unawa
slides.text: Mga Tanong: 1. Ano ang ginagawa ni Langgam habang naglalaro si Tipaklong? 2. Ano ang nangyari kay Tipaklong nang dumating ang tag-ulan? 3. Paano tinulungan ni Langgam si Tipaklong sa dulo ng kwento?

slides.title: Mabuting Aral at Pagninilay
slides.text: Kaisipan: Ang pagiging masipag at handa sa hinaharap ay nagliligtas sa atin sa hirap. May oras para sa paglalaro at may oras para sa pagtatrabaho. Ang pagiging handa ay nagpapakita ng katalinuhan, habang ang pagtulong sa nangangailangan ay nagpapakita ng kabaitan. Talakayin: Ano ang mga aralin sa paaralan na maaari mong pag-aralan nang maaga upang maging handa sa pagsusulit?

slides.title: Kuwento: Si Juan Tamad - Bahagi 1
slides.text: Sa isang maliit na baryo, may isang batang lalaki na nagngangalang Juan. Dahil sa kanyang labis na katamaran, binansagan siya ng kanyang mga kapitbahay na si Juan Tamad. Tuwing inuutusan siya ng kanyang ina, lagi siyang nagdadahilan o gumagawa ng paraan upang hindi matapos ang gawain. Isang mainit na hapon, habang naglalakad si Juan sa tabi ng kalsada, nakakita siya ng isang puno ng bayabas na puno ng malalaki at hinog na bunga. Gustong-gusto niyang kumain ng bayabas, ngunit napakataas ng puno at ayaw niyang magkapagod sa pag-akyat.

slides.title: Kuwento: Si Juan Tamad - Bahagi 2
slides.text: Sa halip na umakyat o kumuha ng mahabang kawayan upang sungkitin ang bayabas, humiga si Juan sa ilalim ng puno. Tumingala siya at tinitigan ang isang napakahinog na bayabas na nakabitin sa mababang sanga. 'Hihiga na lang ako rito at bubuksan ko ang aking bibig. Hihintayin ko na lang na mahulog ang bayabas sa aking bibig,' sabi ni Juan sa sarili. Nanatili siyang nakahiga sa ilalim ng puno ng ilang oras, habang lumilipas ang araw at nagugutom siya. Ang mga ibon naman ay dumapo sa puno at kinain ang ilang bayabas sa itaas habang pinapanood si Juan.

slides.title: Kuwento: Si Juan Tamad - Bahagi 3
slides.text: Nang dumaan ang kanyang ina sa kalsada at nakita si Juan na nakanganga sa ilalim ng puno, napa-iling ito sa lungkot at galit. 'Juan! Bakit ka nakahiga diyan?' tanong ng ina. 'Ina, hinihintay ko pong mahulog ang bayabas sa aking bibig,' sagot ni Juan. Sinabi ng kanyang ina, 'Napakalaking bayabas niyan, Juan! Kung tumayo ka lamang at kinuha ito gamit ang iyong kamay, kanina ka pa busog! Walang pagkain na kusang mahuhulog sa bibig ng taong ayaw gumawa.' Nahiya si Juan at tumayo upang tulungan ang kanyang ina sa pagdadala ng mga kahoy.

slides.title: Talasalitaan at Pag-aaral ng Salita
slides.text: Mga Bokabularyo: • Binansagan - Binigyan ng palayaw o pangalan batay sa ugali. • Sungkitin - Kumuha ng bagay sa itaas gamit ang stick o kawayan. • Kusang - Nangyayari nang walang tulong o kusa ng iba. Tutok sa Phonics: Magsanay sa pagbigkas ng tunog ng 'j' o 'h' sa 'juan', 'hinog', at 'higa'.

slides.title: Mga Tanong sa Pag-unawa
slides.text: Mga Tanong: 1. Bakit binansagan si Juan na 'Juan Tamad'? 2. Paano nakuha ni Juan ang bayabas ayon sa kanyang plano? 3. Ano ang sinabi ng kanyang ina nang makita siyang nakahiga sa ilalim ng puno?

slides.title: Mabuting Aral at Pagninilay
slides.text: Kaisipan: Ang mga pangarap at pangangailangan ay makakamit lamang sa pamamagitan ng pagkilos. Ang katamaran ay nagdadala ng gutom at kawalan ng pag-unlad. Hindi dapat iasa sa tadhana o pagkakataon ang mga bagay na maaari namang pagtrabahuhan. Talakayin: Ano ang mga layunin mo sa buhay na nangangailangan ng iyong pagsisikap upang maabot?

math.title: Mathematics
math.subtitle: Week 3: Parallel, Intersecting, and Perpendicular Lines

slides.title: Lines in Our Environment
slides.text: Let's explore how lines relate to each other in space. Lines can run side-by-side, cross each other, or meet at special angles to form shapes. In math, we express this as: Line relationships = shapes and positions. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Can you look around the room and find where two lines meet?

examples.title: Example 1
examples.content: Review standard details: Line relationships = shapes and positions.

slides.title: What are Parallel Lines?
slides.text: Parallel lines are straight lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart. No matter how far they extend, they will never cross. In math, we express this as: Parallel lines = never meet, constant distance. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Can you draw two parallel lines on your paper using a ruler?

examples.title: Example 2
examples.content: Review standard details: Parallel lines = never meet, constant distance.

slides.title: Parallel Lines in Daily Life
slides.text: We see models of parallel lines everywhere. The rails of a train track, the opposite edges of a ruler, or parallel crop rows in Polomolok farms. In math, we express this as: Crop rows = parallel paths in farms. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Why must train tracks be parallel to each other to be safe?

examples.title: Example 3
examples.content: Review standard details: Crop rows = parallel paths in farms.

slides.title: Recognizing Parallel Lines
slides.text: We can test if two lines are parallel by measuring the perpendicular distance between them at different points. The distance must be exactly the same everywhere. In math, we express this as: Distance d1 = d2 at all points. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. If the distance between two paths changes, are they still parallel?

examples.title: Example 4
examples.content: Review standard details: Distance d1 = d2 at all points.

slides.title: What are Intersecting Lines?
slides.text: Intersecting lines are straight lines that cross or meet each other at a single shared point. We call this crossing point the point of intersection. In math, we express this as: Intersecting lines = cross at a single point. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Can you find where two streets cross to make an intersection?

examples.title: Example 5
examples.content: Review standard details: Intersecting lines = cross at a single point.

slides.title: Finding Intersecting Lines
slides.text: When two lines cross, they form an X-like shape. The point where they meet is a point that lies on both lines at the same time. In math, we express this as: Intersection = shared crossing dot. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. How many points do two straight intersecting lines share with each other?

examples.title: Example 6
examples.content: Review standard details: Intersection = shared crossing dot.

slides.title: What are Perpendicular Lines?
slides.text: Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines. When they cross, they form four equal right angles that look like square corners. In math, we express this as: Perpendicular lines = cross at a right angle. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. What symbol do we use to show that lines are perpendicular?

examples.title: Example 7
examples.content: Review standard details: Perpendicular lines = cross at a right angle.

slides.title: Understanding the Right Angle
slides.text: A right angle is an angle of exactly ninety degrees. It forms a perfect L-shape, like the corner of your classroom blackboard or book cover. In math, we express this as: Right angle = exactly 90 degrees. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. How can you use a piece of paper to check if an angle is right?

examples.title: Example 8
examples.content: Review standard details: Right angle = exactly 90 degrees.

slides.title: Perpendicular Lines in Real Life
slides.text: We see perpendicular lines in many places. Street crossings in Koronadal City, window frames, or the corner joints of a wooden pineapple crate are perpendicular. In math, we express this as: Street corner = 90 degree crossing. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Why are building walls built perpendicular to the flat floor surface?

examples.title: Example 9
examples.content: Review standard details: Street corner = 90 degree crossing.

slides.title: Comparing Line Relationships
slides.text: Let's compare: parallel lines never meet. Intersecting lines meet at any angle. Perpendicular lines meet at exactly a ninety-degree right angle in a corner. In math, we express this as: Parallel vs Intersecting vs Perpendicular. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. We use a standard ruler and a square corner template to draw perpendicular lines accurately. Which type of lines form the corner of a square tile?

examples.title: Example 10
examples.content: Review standard details: Parallel vs Intersecting vs Perpendicular.

slides.title: Drawing Parallel Lines
slides.text: To draw parallel lines, we place our ruler flat, draw one line along the top edge, slide the ruler down, and draw the second line. In math, we express this as: Step-by-step ruler guide. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. We use a standard ruler and a square corner template to draw perpendicular lines accurately. How does a ruler help us keep the distance between lines equal?

examples.title: Example 11
examples.content: Review standard details: Step-by-step ruler guide.

slides.title: Drawing Perpendicular Lines
slides.text: To draw perpendicular lines, we draw a flat horizontal line first, then use a square template or protractor to draw a vertical line straight up. In math, we express this as: Drawing square corner angles. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. We use a standard ruler and a square corner template to draw perpendicular lines accurately. Why is a square corner template helpful when drawing perpendicular paths?

examples.title: Example 12
examples.content: Review standard details: Drawing square corner angles.

slides.title: Drawing Intersecting Lines
slides.text: Drawing intersecting lines is simple. We draw two straight lines that cross each other at any angle, and mark the crossing point with a dot. In math, we express this as: Creating cross line patterns. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Can you label the intersection point of your lines as Point P?

examples.title: Example 13
examples.content: Review standard details: Creating cross line patterns.

slides.title: Drawing Equal Line Segments
slides.text: Let's draw two different line segments of equal length. We use a ruler to draw Segment AB as 6 cm, and Segment CD as 6 cm. In math, we express this as: Length AB = Length CD = 6 cm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Does the direction of the segment change its measured length value?

examples.title: Example 14
examples.content: Review standard details: Length AB = Length CD = 6 cm.

slides.title: Measuring Equal Segments
slides.text: We can verify that two segments are equal by measuring both with a metric ruler. If both measure fifty millimeters, they are equal in length. In math, we express this as: AB = CD = 50 mm. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Why is accurate measurement important when cutting wood to build frames?

examples.title: Example 15
examples.content: Review standard details: AB = CD = 50 mm.

slides.title: Parallel Weaving in Lake Sebu
slides.text: Weavers in Lake Sebu use parallel stripes in their patterns. They align colorful threads side-by-side to create symmetric borders on their T'nalak cloth. In math, we express this as: Parallel stripes = traditional T'nalak design. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. We use a standard ruler and a square corner template to draw perpendicular lines accurately. How do parallel lines make hand-woven patterns look neat and organized?

examples.title: Example 16
examples.content: Review standard details: Parallel stripes = traditional T'nalak design.

slides.title: Grid Layouts in Communities
slides.text: Town planners in Tupi design community grids using parallel and perpendicular streets. This makes it easy to organize blocks of houses and parks. In math, we express this as: Street grids = parallel and perpendicular blocks. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. How does a grid layout help people navigate a town easily?

examples.title: Example 17
examples.content: Review standard details: Street grids = parallel and perpendicular blocks.

slides.title: Finding Corners in Shapes
slides.text: Rectangles and squares are made of perpendicular lines. Every corner of a rectangle is a right angle where two perpendicular sides meet each other. In math, we express this as: Square corners = perpendicular sides of rectangles. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. How many right angles can you find in a standard square?

examples.title: Example 18
examples.content: Review standard details: Square corners = perpendicular sides of rectangles.

slides.title: Line Types in Alphabet Letters
slides.text: Look at alphabet letters: H has parallel and perpendicular lines. X has intersecting lines. T has perpendicular lines. E has parallel and perpendicular lines. In math, we express this as: Letter lines = parallel, intersecting, perpendicular. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Can you find a letter in your name that has parallel lines?

examples.title: Example 19
examples.content: Review standard details: Letter lines = parallel, intersecting, perpendicular.

slides.title: Mount Matutum Trail Maps
slides.text: On a map of Mount Matutum trails, hiking paths represent lines. Some trails run parallel to each other, while others intersect at camp locations. In math, we express this as: Trail crossing = intersecting paths. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. We use a standard ruler and a square corner template to draw perpendicular lines accurately. What does an intersection of two trails represent for hikers?

examples.title: Example 20
examples.content: Review standard details: Trail crossing = intersecting paths.

slides.title: Building Wooden Crates
slides.text: Farmers build wooden crates for pineapples using perpendicular joints. Connecting the wooden boards at ninety-degree right angles makes the crates strong and stable. In math, we express this as: Crate joints = perpendicular corner connections. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. We use a standard ruler and a square corner template to draw perpendicular lines accurately. What would happen if the crate corners were not perpendicular joints?

examples.title: Example 21
examples.content: Review standard details: Crate joints = perpendicular corner connections.

slides.title: Common Error: Non-Parallel Lines
slides.text: A common error is drawing two lines that look parallel but are slightly tilted. If extended far enough, these lines will eventually intersect each other. In math, we express this as: Non-parallel lines = eventually cross in distance. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. Why must we measure the distance at both ends to confirm parallel lines?

examples.title: Example 22
examples.content: Review standard details: Non-parallel lines = eventually cross in distance.

slides.title: Common Error: Tilted Intersections
slides.text: Another mistake is drawing intersecting lines that look perpendicular but are slightly tilted. We must verify they form a true ninety-degree angle using a corner. In math, we express this as: Tilted crossing = not perpendicular. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. We use a standard ruler and a square corner template to draw perpendicular lines accurately. How can you verify if two crossed lines are perpendicular?

examples.title: Example 23
examples.content: Review standard details: Tilted crossing = not perpendicular.

slides.title: Line Relationship Exercises
slides.text: Let's practice identifying lines. Look at different pairs of lines on your worksheet and classify them as parallel, intersecting, or perpendicular based on their crossings. In math, we express this as: Classify line pairs = parallel, perpendicular, intersecting. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. What type of lines do the hands of a clock make at three?

examples.title: Example 24
examples.content: Review standard details: Classify line pairs = parallel, perpendicular, intersecting.

slides.title: Summary of Week 3 Lessons
slides.text: Let's review: we learned to identify and draw parallel lines, intersecting lines, and perpendicular lines, and to draw equal line segments using a metric ruler. In math, we express this as: Line types = parallel, perpendicular, and intersecting. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Parallel lines are lines on a flat surface that always stay the same distance apart and never cross. Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at a single shared point called the intersection. Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross to form a right angle. A right angle is exactly ninety degrees, which looks like the square corner of a book or sheet of paper. How do these line relationships help us draw shapes accurately?

examples.title: Example 25
examples.content: Review standard details: Line types = parallel, perpendicular, and intersecting.

pages:
Exercise 1: Identifying Types of Lines

Write Parallel, Intersecting, or Perpendicular for each pair of lines below.

1. Lines that cross and form four square corners:
Perpendicular
2. Lines that run side-by-side and never cross:
Parallel

Exercise 2: Drawing Equal Line Segments

Answer the questions about ruler measurements and line segment lengths.

1. To draw a line segment of exactly 8 cm, where should you place the start point on the ruler?
0 mark

Exercise 3: Geometry in Art

Identify geometric line patterns found in local South Cotabato arts.

1. What type of line patterns are formed by the crossing warp and weft fibers in T'nalak weaving?
Intersecting

performance.title: [Advanced] Street Map Designer with Lines
performance.desc: Advanced Task: On a clean sheet of paper, design a simple road map for a barangay in Polomolok. Draw at least two parallel roads (label them Pineapple Road and Matutum Road). Draw another road that intersects both parallel roads (label it Mountain Road). Finally, draw a road that is perpendicular to Pineapple Road (label it Market Road). Draw two equal-sized houses using your ruler. Take a photo of your labeled map and upload it here.
labels: Correct parallel and perpendicular roads drawing
Map labeled correctly
Correct drawing of two equal-sized houses using a ruler

slides.title: Advanced Instrumentation
slides.text: Scientific research relies on high-precision instruments that extend human senses and reduce measurement error. For instance, consider the example of Calibration: Comparing instrument readouts against a known standard to adjust accuracy. For instance, consider the example of Precision: The repeatability of measurements under unchanged conditions. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

slides.title: Advanced Vocabulary
slides.text: Terms of precision measurement: Graduated cylinder, Triple beam balance, Calipers, and Meniscus. For instance, consider the example of Graduated cylinder: A narrow container with accurate marks used to measure fluid volumes. For instance, consider the example of Meniscus: The curve at the upper surface of a liquid in a tube. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

slides.title: Instrument Selection Strategy
slides.text: Selecting tools requires matching the scale and physical properties of the subject with the resolution of the instrument. For instance, consider the example of Resolution: The smallest increment of change that can be detected by a tool. For instance, consider the example of Tolerance: The allowable limit of variation in a physical measurement. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. slides.text: Vernier calipers measure internal, external, and depth dimensions with sub-millimeter precision. For instance, consider the example of External Jaws: Measuring the diameter of a clay sphere. For instance, consider the example of Vernier Scale: Reading the fractional division of a millimeter for high accuracy. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. slides.title: Advanced Tool: Anemometer
slides.text: Anemometers measure wind speed and direction, converting wind pressure into mechanical rotation. For instance, consider the example of Cups: Three or four spinning cups capture wind velocity. For instance, consider the example of Digital Readout: Displays speed in kilometers per hour or knots. For instance, consider the example of Floatation: Floats higher in dense liquids like saltwater, lower in pure water. For instance, consider the example of Scale: Readings at the point where the liquid surface cuts the hydrometer stem. slides.title: Common Error: Parallax Error
slides.text: Parallax error happens when you read markings from an angle instead of eye level. For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Looking down at a thermometer creates a false higher reading. For instance, consider the example of Correct: Position your eyes exactly level with the fluid surface. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. examples.content: Looking down at a thermometer creates a false higher reading.

examples.title: Correct
examples.content: Position your eyes exactly level with the fluid surface.

slides.title: Common Error: Taring Neglect
slides.text: Neglecting to tare a scale results in the container's weight being added to the mass. For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Weighing 10g of salt inside a 50g beaker and recording 60g. For instance, consider the example of Correct: Placing the beaker, pressing 'Tare' (zeroing), then adding salt for a 10g reading. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. standard.q: Which tool is specifically designed to measure fluid volume with the highest precision?
What do we call the curved surface of a liquid in a narrow glass tube?
How do you calculate the total magnification of a microscope using a 10x eyepiece and a 40x objective lens?
What error occurs when a student reads the liquid scale from an angle rather than at eye level?
Which function on a digital scale resets the reading to zero after placing a container?
What force unit is measured by a spring scale?
Which tool is used to measure wind speed in meteorology?
How does a hydrometer work to measure liquid density?
options: It measures the temperature of the liquid.
It floats at different depths based on liquid buoyancy.
It weighs the liquid on a scale.
It counts the drops of liquid.

standard.q: Which instrument is used to measure atmospheric pressure to predict weather?
What should you do to a digital scale before weighing a substance?
options: Tare it to read exactly zero.

standard.q: What is the resolution of a ruler that shows markings for every millimeter?
Why does sound travel fastest through solid metals?
options: Molecules are packed tightly together, transferring waves rapidly.

standard.q: Which tool is used to transfer very small liquid volumes in microliters?
What is the purpose of calibrating a pH meter with buffer solutions?
options: To adjust the accuracy of the reading against known values.
To increase the viscosity of the water.

standard.q: True or False: Mercury forms a convex meniscus, meaning the curve points upwards.
Which tool is used to measure the intensity of sound in decibels?
What does a spectrophotometer measure?
Why are photocell gate timers preferred over hand-held stopwatches for speed tests?
options: They eliminate human reaction time delays.
They make the cars go faster.

standard.q: Which tool would you use to measure the thickness of a leaf with sub-millimeter precision?
What safety tool is used to carry a hot crucible from a Bunsen burner?
True or False: Insufficient calibration leads to systemic errors in experimental results.
What happens to the meniscus curve when reading water volume in a plastic cylinder?
options: It is almost flat because water does not adhere strongly to plastic.

standard.q: What adjustment knob is used to sharpen focus under high microscope magnification?
Which tool measures specific gravity of liquids?
What unit is displayed on a spring scale?

challenge.q: A student records the volume of water in a graduated cylinder as 45 mL. If they looked down at the cylinder from an angle, what is their error called, and is the actual volume higher or lower?
options: Parallax error; actual volume is lower than 45 mL.
Parallax error; actual volume is higher than 45 mL.
Zeroing error; actual volume is exactly 45 mL.
Calibration error; actual volume is 45 mL.

challenge.q: You are using a Vernier caliper to measure the outer diameter of a marble. The main scale reads 1.4 cm, and the Vernier scale aligns at the 3rd mark. What is the diameter?
Explain why a triple beam balance is calibrated to zero before usage.
options: To prevent rust from forming on the beams.
To ensure the mass readings are accurate and not shifted by tare values.
To make the pans balance faster.
To prevent the weights from sliding.

challenge.q: If you need to measure exactly 2.5 microliters of a enzyme solution, which tool is required?
A student weighs 5 grams of salt in a beaker without taring the scale. The beaker weighs 42 grams. What does the scale read?

performance.title: Precision Calibration & Measurement Design
performance.desc: Write a step-by-step protocol for calibrating a digital kitchen scale at home. Explain what parallax error is and draw three diagrams: one showing a graduated cylinder read from too high, one read from too low, and one read correctly at eye level. Label the meniscus in each diagram.
labels: Wrote an accurate step-by-step digital scale calibration protocol
Clearly explained parallax error and how to avoid it
Drew three detailed, labeled diagrams comparing eye levels and meniscus reading

Microliters (uL)

Tool Name Calibration Standard Action Required
pH Meter pH 7.0 buffer solution Submerge probe and press Calibrate
Digital Scale 100g calibration weight Zero scale, place weight, adjust
Spring Scale Empty vertical hang Turn adjustment screw until line is at 0

english.title: English
english.subtitle: Week 3: Collective Nouns & Final Consonant Blends

slides.title: What are Collective Nouns?
slides.text: A collective noun is a word that names a group of people, animals, or things as a single unit or entity. Even though the group has many members, we treat the noun as a single thing. For instance, consider the example of For People: A 'class' of students, a 'team' of players, an 'audience' of listeners. For instance, consider the example of For Animals: A 'flock' of birds, a 'herd' of cows, a 'school' of fish, a 'pack' of wolves. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: For People
examples.content: A 'class' of students, a 'team' of players, an 'audience' of listeners.

examples.title: For Animals
examples.content: A 'flock' of birds, a 'herd' of cows, a 'school' of fish, a 'pack' of wolves.

slides.title: Collective Nouns for Things
slides.text: We also use specific collective nouns to group inanimate objects together. Using the correct collective noun makes your English descriptive and precise. For instance, consider the example of Everyday Objects: A 'bunch' of keys, a 'set' of tools, a 'pack' of cards, a 'fleet' of ships. For instance, consider the example of In a Sentence: Matteo found a 'bunch' of bananas on the kitchen counter. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications.

examples.title: Everyday Objects
examples.content: A 'bunch' of keys, a 'set' of tools, a 'pack' of cards, a 'fleet' of ships.

examples.title: In a Sentence
examples.content: Matteo found a 'bunch' of bananas on the kitchen counter.

slides.title: Agreement Rules: Singular vs. Plural
slides.text: Most of the time, collective nouns take singular verbs because we view the group acting together as one unit. However, if the members are acting individually, we can use a plural verb. For instance, consider the example of Singular Verb (Together): The team 'is' playing well. (The group acts together -> Singular) For instance, consider the example of Plural Verb (Individuals): The family 'are' arguing about their dinner plans. (Members act separately -> Plural) To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Singular Verb (Together)
examples.content: The team 'is' playing well. (The group acts together -> Singular)

examples.title: Plural Verb (Individuals)
examples.content: The family 'are' arguing about their dinner plans. (Members act separately -> Plural)

slides.title: What are Final Consonant Blends?
slides.text: A final consonant blend is when two consonant letters sit at the end of a word, and both consonant sounds are pronounced smoothly together. Let's practice hearing and spelling these endings. For instance, consider the example of The -st Blend: /s/ and /t/ blend to make /st/ as in 'nest', 'fast', or 'vest'. For instance, consider the example of The -nd Blend: /n/ and /d/ blend to make /nd/ as in 'hand', 'sand', or 'pond'. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: The -st Blend
examples.content: /s/ and /t/ blend to make /st/ as in 'nest', 'fast', or 'vest'.

examples.title: The -nd Blend
examples.content: /n/ and /d/ blend to make /nd/ as in 'hand', 'sand', or 'pond'.

slides.title: Common Final Blends: -nd and -mp
slides.text: Practice saying and spelling: -nd (wind, send, land, mind) and -mp (camp, jump, lamp, stamp). Listen carefully to make sure you hear both ending sounds. For instance, consider the example of -nd Sentences: Matteo played on the wet 'sand' near the 'pond'. For instance, consider the example of -mp Sentences: Turn off the 'lamp' before you 'jump' into bed. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications.

examples.title: -nd Sentences
examples.content: Matteo played on the wet 'sand' near the 'pond'.

examples.title: -mp Sentences
examples.content: Turn off the 'lamp' before you 'jump' into bed.

slides.title: Common Final Blends: -st and -ft
slides.text: Practice saying: -st (dust, last, post, best) and -ft (gift, lift, soft, draft). S-blends and T-blends are very sharp at the end of words. For instance, consider the example of -st Sentences: She was the 'last' student to finish the reading 'test'. For instance, consider the example of -ft Sentences: He gave his mother a lovely 'gift' wrapped in 'soft' paper. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications.

examples.title: -st Sentences
examples.content: She was the 'last' student to finish the reading 'test'.

examples.title: -ft Sentences
examples.content: He gave his mother a lovely 'gift' wrapped in 'soft' paper.

slides.title: Common Final Blends: -lk and -lt
slides.text: Practice saying: -lk (milk, walk, talk) and -lt (belt, melt, salt). These blends contain the letter 'l' and have a soft, flowing sound followed by a sharp ending. For instance, consider the example of -lk Sentences: Drink a glass of cold 'milk' every morning. For instance, consider the example of -lt Sentences: The ice will 'melt' quickly in the hot sun. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications.

examples.title: -lk Sentences
examples.content: Drink a glass of cold 'milk' every morning.

examples.title: -lt Sentences
examples.content: The ice will 'melt' quickly in the hot sun.

slides.title: Analyzing Text: Cause & Effect
slides.text: Informational texts often show Cause and Effect. The Cause is *why* something happens (the reason). The Effect is *what* happens (the result). Look for clue words like: because, so, since, as a result. For instance, consider the example of Identifying Cause: Because the sun was hot, the ice cream melted. Cause: Sun was hot. For instance, consider the example of Identifying Effect: Because the sun was hot, the ice cream melted. Effect: Ice cream melted. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Identifying Cause
examples.content: Because the sun was hot, the ice cream melted. Cause: Sun was hot.

examples.title: Identifying Effect
examples.content: Because the sun was hot, the ice cream melted. Effect: Ice cream melted.

slides.title: Karagdagang Pagsasanay 1
slides.text: Mag-review tayo ng mga aralin sa linggong ito. Pinag-aralan natin ang mahahalagang konsepto ng Week 3. Maging maingat sa pagbabasa at pag-unawa ng mga salita. Gamitin ang iyong kaalaman sa iyong sariling buhay. Maaari mong talakayin ito kasama ang iyong mga kalaro. Ang pag-aaral ng wika at asal ay nagpapalaki sa atin. Subukang sagutin ang lahat ng tanong sa pagsusulit natin. Ipagpatuloy ang pagiging mahusay at masipag na mag-aaral! For instance, consider the example of Pagsasanay 1: Ibahagi ang iyong mga natutunan sa aralin sa iyong pamilya ngayon.
